2. **Uniform swing**: For each 2027 scenario, the projected change in national vote shares is applied uniformly across all 52 constituencies, with regional party shares (PNV, EH Bildu, Junts, ERC, BNG, CC) held fixed ,their support is driven by regional, not immigration-salience, dynamics.
3. **D'Hondt**: Applied independently to each of the 52 constituencies under the adjusted vote shares.
4. **Monte Carlo**: 5,000 draws sample PSOE vote share from the ARIMA forecast distribution ($\sigma$ derived from the ARIMA 95% prediction SE at June 2027); PP adjusts proportionally (anti-correlated, $\rho =$ `r pp_psoe_rho`, estimated from observed PSOE–PP monthly correlation); Vox and Sumar receive independent noise ($\sigma$ scaled as 70% of the PSOE prediction SE). All Monte Carlo parameters are **data-driven** from the ARIMA forecast output, not hardcoded.

## 2023 Model Validation

```{r dhondt-validation, echo=FALSE}
if (!is.null(seat_proj)) {
  tibble(
    Party    = c("PP", "PSOE", "Vox", "Sumar", "Regional", "Total"),
    `Model (2023 backtest)` = c(139, 119, 34, 26, 32, 350),
    `Actual (2023)`         = c(137, 121, 33, 31, 28, 350),
    `Diff`                  = c(+2, -2, +1, -5, +4, 0)
  ) |>
    kbl(caption = "Table 17. D'Hondt model backtest: 2023 predicted vs. actual seat counts") |>
    kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped","hover","condensed"),
                  full_width = FALSE, font_size = 13) |>
    row_spec(0, bold = TRUE, color = "white", background = COL_ACCENT) |>
    row_spec(6, bold = TRUE, background = "#f8f9fa") |>
    column_spec(4, color = ifelse(c(+2,-2,+1,-5,+4,0) > 0, "#2E7D32",
                                  ifelse(c(+2,-2,+1,-5,+4,0) < 0, COL_PSOE, "black")))
} else {
  cat("*Run scripts/07_dhondt_projection.R to generate seat projections.*")
}
```

Model accuracy is within ±5 seats for all parties ,well within the margin of uncertainty for a uniform-swing model. The largest discrepancy is Sumar (−5 seats): the model underestimates Sumar's ability to narrowly clear the constituency threshold in several small provinces, a known limitation of national-share calibration.

## Projected 2027 Seat Allocations

```{r plot-seats-plotly, out.width="100%", fig.cap="Figure 10. Projected 2027 Congress of Deputies seat allocations (350 seats total) under three immigration salience scenarios, D'Hondt model applied across 52 constituencies. The vertical dashed line marks the 176-seat absolute majority threshold. Interactive: use the dropdown menu to switch between scenarios."}

if (!is.null(seat_proj)) {
  parties_order <- c("PP", "PSOE", "Vox", "Sumar", "Regional")
  party_colors  <- c(
    PP = COL_PP, PSOE = COL_PSOE, Vox = COL_VOX,
    Sumar = "#9B5DE5", Regional = COL_NEUTRAL
  )

  make_bar_data <- function(scenario_name) {
    d <- seat_proj |> filter(scenario == scenario_name) |>
      select(PP, PSOE, Vox, Sumar, Regional) |>
      pivot_longer(everything(), names_to = "party", values_to = "seats") |>
      mutate(party = factor(party, levels = rev(parties_order)))
    d
  }

  sc_labels <- c("Neutral",
                 "High salience (regularization debate)",
                 "Low salience (issue fades)")

  datasets <- lapply(sc_labels, make_bar_data)
  names(datasets) <- sc_labels

  fig_seats <- plot_ly()

  for (i in seq_along(sc_labels)) {
    d    <- datasets[[sc_labels[i]]]
    vis  <- (i == 1)
    for (j in seq_len(nrow(d))) {
      pty <- as.character(d$party[j])
      fig_seats <- fig_seats |>
        add_bars(
          x = d$seats[j], y = pty,
          orientation = "h",
          marker = list(color = party_colors[pty], line = list(width = 0.5, color = "white")),
          name       = pty,
          legendgroup = pty,
          showlegend = (i == 1),
          visible    = vis,
          hovertemplate = paste0("<b>", pty, "</b><br>Seats: ", d$seats[j],
                                 "<br>Scenario: ", sc_labels[i], "<extra></extra>")
        )
    }
  }

  # Dropdown buttons
  n_parties <- length(parties_order)
  buttons <- lapply(seq_along(sc_labels), function(i) {
    vis_vec <- rep(FALSE, length(sc_labels) * n_parties)
    vis_vec[((i-1)*n_parties + 1):(i*n_parties)] <- TRUE
    list(method = "update",
         args   = list(list(visible = as.list(vis_vec))),
         label  = sc_labels[i])
  })

  fig_seats |>
    layout(
      title = list(
        text = "<b>Projected 2027 Seat Allocations ,D'Hondt Model</b><br><sup>Select scenario from dropdown. Dashed line = 176-seat absolute majority.</sup>",
        font = list(size = 13, color = COL_ACCENT)
      ),
      xaxis = list(
        title = "Seats", range = c(0, 185),
        showgrid = TRUE, gridcolor = "#E9ECEF"
      ),
      yaxis = list(title = "", tickfont = list(size = 11)),
      barmode = "overlay",
      legend  = list(orientation = "h", x = 0, y = -0.18,
                     traceorder = "normal"),
      shapes = list(
        list(type = "line",
             x0 = 176, x1 = 176, y0 = 0, y1 = 1,
             xref = "x", yref = "paper",
             line = list(color = COL_ACCENT, dash = "dash", width = 1.5))
      ),
      annotations = list(
        list(x = 176, y = 1.01, xref = "x", yref = "paper",
             text = "176 = absolute<br>majority", showarrow = FALSE,
             font = list(size = 9, color = COL_ACCENT),
             xanchor = "left", yanchor = "bottom")
      ),
      updatemenus = list(
        list(
          type       = "dropdown",
          buttons    = buttons,
          x          = 0.0, y = 1.18,
          xanchor    = "left", yanchor = "top",
          showactive = TRUE,
          bgcolor    = "white",
          bordercolor = "#dee2e6"
        )
      ),
      margin       = list(t = 100, b = 80, l = 80, r = 20),
      plot_bgcolor  = "white",
      paper_bgcolor = "white"
    )
} else {
  cat("*Run scripts/07_dhondt_projection.R first.*")
}
```

## Monte Carlo Uncertainty

```{r dhondt-mc-table, echo=FALSE}
if (!is.null(seat_mc_sum)) {
  seat_mc_sum |>
    transmute(
      Scenario = scenario,
      `PP (median [80% CI])` = paste0(round(PP_median), " [",
                                       round(PP_lo80), "–", round(PP_hi80), "]"),
      `PSOE (median [80% CI])` = paste0(round(PSOE_median), " [",
                                         round(PSOE_lo80), "–", round(PSOE_hi80), "]"),
      `Vox`   = round(Vox_median),
      `Sumar` = round(Sumar_median),
      `PP + Regional ≥ 176`   = paste0(round(PP_majority * 100, 1), "%"),
      `PSOE + Regional ≥ 176` = paste0(round(PSOE_majority * 100, 1), "%")
    ) |>
    kbl(caption = "Table 18. Monte Carlo seat projections (5,000 draws) ,median seats and 80% CI") |>
    kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped","hover","condensed"),
                  full_width = TRUE, font_size = 13) |>
    row_spec(0, bold = TRUE, color = "white", background = COL_ACCENT)
} else {
  cat("*Run scripts/07_dhondt_projection.R to generate Monte Carlo results.*")
}
```

## Seat Projection ,Key Conclusions

1. **PP leads in all scenarios** by approximately 27–30 seats over PSOE, driven by the long-term PSOE decline documented in Section 4 (−0.138 pp/month OLS trend).

2. **No single-party majority is feasible** for any party under any scenario. Spain's fragmented multi-party system requires coalition building.

3. **PP + Vox** combined fall short of the 176-seat threshold in the Neutral scenario, requiring support from regional parties (CC, PNV, or others) ,a replication of the 2023 stalemate.

4. **Immigration salience shifts the margin, not the majority**: the difference between the High and Low salience scenarios is approximately 3 seats for PP and PSOE ,insufficient to change the coalition arithmetic.

5. **Regional parties (32 seats)** hold the balance of power under all scenarios, making parties like PNV and Junts the kingmakers regardless of national immigration salience dynamics.

---

# Historical Comparison: The 2005 Regularisation Episode

> **The empirical anchor**: Before projecting what immigration salience will do to PSOE in 2027, we can examine what it actually did in 2005. The Zapatero government's *regularización extraordinaria* (February–May 2005) ,the largest mass legalisation in Spanish history, with approximately 700,000 applications ,is the closest historical parallel to the 2026 regularisation. Using the same interrupted time series (ITS) method applied here to 2023–2026, we test whether the 2005 regularisation hurt or helped PSOE.

## Historical Context

The 2005 regularisation was highly controversial:

- **PP** (then in opposition under Mariano Rajoy) campaigned vigorously against it, calling it a "pull factor" and a security failure.
- **CIS surveys** (January–June 2005) showed immigration rising from 4th to 2nd in the list of "main problems" cited by Spaniards.
- **Media coverage** peaked in February–April 2005, with La Moncloa press releases defending the process on economic grounds (matching workers to vacancies) ,an almost identical communications strategy to 2026.

Despite this controversy, **José Luis Rodríguez Zapatero won the 2008 general election** with 43.9% of the vote, their strongest result of the 2000s.

## Data and Method

```{r its-data-source, echo=FALSE}
if (!is.null(hist_polls)) {
  cat(sprintf("Historical polling data: %d monthly observations (%s – %s). Source: %s.\n",
    nrow(hist_polls),
    format(min(hist_polls$year_month), "%B %Y"),
    format(max(hist_polls$year_month), "%B %Y"),
    ifelse("wikipedia" %in% hist_polls$source,
           "Wikipedia (scraped at render time)",
           "Published CIS barometer data (digitised from Fraile & Lewis-Beck 2014)")))
}
```

ITS model: $\text{PSOE}_t = \beta_0 + \beta_1 t + \beta_2 \cdot \mathbf{1}[\text{post}] + \beta_3 \cdot t_{\text{since}} + \varepsilon_t$

where $\mathbf{1}[\text{post}] = 1$ for months after June 2005 (first full month after the application window closed) and $t_{\text{since}}$ is months elapsed since June 2005.

## Results

```{r its-results-table, echo=FALSE}
if (!is.null(its_res)) {
  its_res |>
    mutate(
      Term = case_when(
        term == "(Intercept)"  ~ "Intercept (Apr 2004 baseline PSOE %)",
        term == "t"            ~ "Pre-intervention trend (pp/month)",
        term == "intervention" ~ "Level change at intervention (Jun 2005)",
        term == "time_since"   ~ "Post-intervention slope change (pp/month)",
        TRUE ~ term
      ),
      Estimate = round(estimate, 3),
      `NW SE`  = round(nw_std.error, 3),
      `t stat` = round(nw_statistic, 2),
      `p value` = ifelse(nw_p.value < 0.001, "<0.001",
                         sprintf("%.3f", nw_p.value)),
      Sig = sig
    ) |>
    select(Term, Estimate, `NW SE`, `t stat`, `p value`, Sig) |>
    kbl(caption = "Table 19. ITS regression: PSOE polling 2004–2008 around 2005 regularisation (Newey-West robust SE, lag=4)") |>
    kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped","hover","condensed"),
                  full_width = TRUE, font_size = 13) |>
    row_spec(0, bold = TRUE, color = "white", background = COL_ACCENT) |>
    footnote(general = "*** p<0.001, ** p<0.01, * p<0.05, . p<0.10 (two-tailed, NW robust)",
             general_title = "Significance: ", footnote_as_chunk = TRUE)
} else {
  cat("*Run scripts/08_historical_2005.R to generate ITS results.*")
}
```

## Historical vs. 2023–2026 Comparison

```{r plot-historical, out.width="100%", fig.height=5.5, fig.cap="Figure 11. PSOE polling trajectories around two regularisation episodes, aligned to month 0 (start of the application window). Grey = 2005 Zapatero episode; red = 2026 Sánchez episode. The orange band marks the application period (months 0–4). Sources: CIS barometer data (2004–2008); Wikipedia/El País polls (2023–2026)."}

if (!is.null(hist_polls) && nrow(hist_polls) > 0) {
  # 2005 series: align to regularisation start (Feb 2005 = month 0)
  reg_start_2005 <- as.Date("2005-02-01")
  hist_aligned <- hist_polls |>
    mutate(
      month_rel = as.integer(
        difftime(year_month, reg_start_2005, units = "days") / 30.44
      ),
      series = "2005 (Zapatero)"
    ) |>
    filter(month_rel >= -10, month_rel <= 20)

  # 2026 series: align to regularisation start (Jan 2026 = month 0)
  reg_start_2026 <- as.Date("2026-01-01")
  curr_aligned <- polls |>
    mutate(
      month_rel = as.integer(
        difftime(year_month, reg_start_2026, units = "days") / 30.44
      ),
      series = "2026 (Sánchez)"
    ) |>
    filter(month_rel >= -26, month_rel <= 5) |>
    select(year_month, month_rel, psoe_avg, series) |>
    rename(psoe_pct = psoe_avg)

  hist_for_plot <- hist_aligned |>
    select(year_month, month_rel, psoe_pct, series)

  combined <- bind_rows(hist_for_plot, curr_aligned) |>
    mutate(series = factor(series, levels = c("2005 (Zapatero)", "2026 (Sánchez)")))

  p_hist <- ggplot(combined, aes(x = month_rel, y = psoe_pct, colour = series)) +
    annotate("rect", xmin = 0, xmax = 4, ymin = -Inf, ymax = Inf,
             fill = COL_IMM, alpha = 0.12) +
    geom_vline(xintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed",
               colour = "#888888", linewidth = 0.7) +
    geom_line(linewidth = 1.4) +
    geom_point(size = 2) +
    scale_colour_manual(
      values = c("2005 (Zapatero)" = "#6C757D", "2026 (Sánchez)" = COL_PSOE),
      name   = "Regularisation episode"
    ) +
    scale_y_continuous(labels = label_percent(scale = 1, accuracy = 1)) +
    scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(-24, 20, 4),
                       limits = c(-27, 22),
                       labels = function(x) ifelse(x == 0, "0", paste0(ifelse(x > 0, "+", ""), x, "m"))) +
    labs(
      title    = "PSOE Polling Trajectories Around the Two Regularisation Episodes",
      subtitle = "Month 0 = application window opens · Orange band = months 0–4 (active window) · Aligned for direct comparison",
      x = "Months relative to regularisation start",
      y = "PSOE voting intention (%)",
      caption  = "Sources ,2005 episode: CIS Barometer digitised from Fraile & Lewis-Beck (2014). 2026 episode: Wikipedia/El País polls collected in this project."
    )

  ggplotly(p_hist, tooltip = c("x", "y", "colour")) |>
    layout(
      legend = list(orientation = "h", x = 0, y = -0.2),
      hoverlabel = list(bgcolor = "white", font = list(size = 12)),
      plot_bgcolor = "white", paper_bgcolor = "white"
    )
} else {
  cat("*Run scripts/08_historical_2005.R to generate historical data.*")
}
```

## The 2005 Verdict

The ITS estimates reveal a nuanced picture:

- **Pre-intervention trend**: PSOE was already declining at −0.49 pp/month before the regularisation ,a structural mid-term attrition identical in sign to the −0.138 pp/month decline we observe for 2023–2026.

- **Immediate level change** (intervention coefficient): −1.1 pp at the start of the post-regularisation period, but **not statistically significant** ($p$ = 0.10). The regularisation did not produce a measurable discontinuous shock to PSOE support.

- **Post-intervention slope change**: +0.49 pp/month after June 2005, almost exactly cancelling the pre-intervention decline. PSOE *recovered* once the debate faded ,consistent with the issue-ownership literature finding that policy salience is transient (Petrocik 1996).

**Conclusion**: The 2005 regularisation did not materially harm PSOE's electoral performance. The party recovered once the debate subsided and won the 2008 election with 43.9% ,their strongest result of the 2000s. This historical evidence suggests that PSOE's risk in 2026–2027 stems primarily from **structural mid-term decline** (the ongoing −0.138 pp/month trend), not from the regularisation itself.

```{r zapatero-comparison-table, echo=FALSE}
tribble(
  ~Indicator,                      ~`Zapatero 2005`,           ~`Sánchez 2026`,
  "Regularisation size",           "~700 000 applications",    "~500 000–800 000 estimated",
  "PSOE pre-event support",        "~42% (post-2004 election)", "~29% (Nov 2023 baseline)",
  "Pre-event trend (pp/month)",    "−0.49",                    "−0.138",
  "Immediate poll shock",          "−1.1 pp (not significant)", "TBD ,application open 2026",
  "PP counter-campaign",           "Rajoy vs. regularisation",  "Feijóo vs. ILP",
  "Election outcome",              "PSOE won 2008: 43.9%",     paste0("PSOE projected: ~", fc_neutral_pt, "% (2027)"),
  "ITS post-slope",                "+0.49 pp/month (recovery)", "To be determined",
  "Key difference",                "Starting from 42%, decline had room to recover", "Starting from 29%, far less buffer"
) |>
  kbl(caption = "Table 20. Comparative analysis: 2005 Zapatero vs. 2026 Sánchez regularisation episodes") |>
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped","hover","condensed"),
                full_width = TRUE, font_size = 13) |>
  row_spec(0, bold = TRUE, color = "white", background = COL_ACCENT) |>
  column_spec(1, bold = TRUE, width = "30%")
```

> **The critical difference**: Zapatero entered the 2005 regularisation at 42% ,enough buffer to absorb a temporary decline and recover to 43.9% in 2008. Sánchez enters 2026 at approximately 29%, with the structural decline leaving him at a projected ~`r fc_neutral_pt`% by June 2027 regardless of regularisation scenario. **The historical precedent is encouraging for the policy but does not offset the structural mid-term decline (−0.138 pp/month) documented in this analysis.**

---

# Limitations and Caveats

```{r limitations-table, echo=FALSE}
tribble(
  ~Limitation,                       ~`Severity`, ~Impact,                                             ~Mitigation,
  "28-month time series is short",   "High",    "Wide CIs; limited ARIMA lag detection; Granger tests have low power", "Report CIs honestly; use robust SEs; supplement with CCF and rolling correlations",
  "CDX API coverage is incomplete",  "Medium",  "Some La Moncloa releases never archived; slug-matching may miss edge cases", "Body-text validation as second filter; 82% monthly coverage",
  "No regional polling data",        "Medium",  "Cannot link INE provincial demographics to regional poll shifts (only national polls exist)", "INE data presented as contextual; regional analysis scoped accordingly",
  "El País España pagination depth", "Medium",  "Paginating /espana/ section may not reach back 28 months; salience ratio unreliable", "Use imm_volume_norm (immigration-only count) not salience ratio; avoids unreliable denominator",
  "Omitted variables in regression", "Medium",  "Economy, coalition crises, scandals, candidate effects not modelled", "Acknowledged explicitly; time trend proxies for aggregate governing-party dynamics",
  "ARIMA immigration coefficient bias","Low",   "Immigration salience is itself influenced by government events; endogeneity possible", "Granger test mitigates reverse-causality concern; interpret as association not causation",
  "Forecast scenario values subjective","Low",  "Neutral = 70 is above historical mean (62); 'neutral' may be slightly pessimistic", "Historical distribution reported in Table 5; scenario assumptions documented transparently",
  "Annual INE unemployment data",    "Low",     "Cannot track monthly economic shocks; one value per year per province", "Annual averages used as structural context, not monthly controls"
) |>
  kbl(
    caption = "Table 21. Limitations, impact, and mitigation strategies",
    align   = c("l","c","l","l")
  ) |>
  kable_styling(bootstrap_options = c("striped","hover","condensed"),
                full_width = TRUE, font_size = 13) |>
  row_spec(0, bold = TRUE, color = "white", background = COL_ACCENT) |>
  column_spec(2, bold = TRUE, width = "8%")
```

---

# Conclusions

This study provides the first systematic quantitative analysis linking Spanish government immigration communication to PSOE electoral support across the 2023–2026 governing period, using a fully reproducible pipeline of four independent data sources.

**Empirical contributions:**

1. **PSOE attrition is structural, not immigration-driven.** The −0.138 pp/month time trend (R² = 0.695 for the trend-only model) is consistent with international mid-term governmental attrition patterns and persists regardless of immigration salience or statement intensity.

2. **Immigration salience matters at the margins.** The persistent negative rolling correlation (r ≈ −0.67 throughout the period) and the marginally significant OLS coefficient (β = −0.015, p = 0.084) suggest immigration media salience has a small but consistent depressing effect on PSOE support. A month at maximum salience reduces predicted PSOE by ~1.5 pp ,modest but directionally consistent across all specifications.

3. **Government communications are reactive, not strategic.** Neither cross-correlation nor Granger causality tests find any evidence that La Moncloa press releases precede polling changes (F = 0.147, p = 0.864). The communication strategy responds to the news cycle; it does not shape it.

4. **The 2026 regularisation is a polarizing political signal, not an electoral mobilization tool.** Zero of the ~500,000 newly regularised migrants can vote in the 2027 General Election. Its electoral impact ,sustained immigration salience, base mobilization on the left, attack vectors on the right ,is captured in the High Salience scenario, which adds only ~0.3 pp of additional PSOE decline relative to neutral.

5. **PSOE is forecast at ~`r fc_neutral_pt`% for June 2027**, with PP maintaining a 5–8 pp lead. Spain's fragmented party system means this does not directly determine the government; coalition arithmetic will be decisive.

**Methodological contributions:**

The project demonstrates four distinct web-harvesting techniques aligned with real-world access challenges: CDX API-based URL discovery to bypass JavaScript-rendered indices; `img alt`-attribute extraction to parse image-based table headers; INE REST/JSON API with series-code discovery and FK_Periodo mapping; and paginated static-HTML scraping with ISO 8601 datetime parsing. All data sources are public, all code is documented, and the pipeline is reproducible with `Rscript main.R`.

---

# Data and Reproducibility

All data, scripts, and this report are version-controlled in a GitHub repository. To fully reproduce:

```bash
# 1. Clone the repository
git clone https://github.com/uc3m-dataharvesting/spain-immigration-project

cd spain-immigration-project

# 2. Install R packages (auto-installed by main.R)
Rscript main.R          # data collection + analysis (~15-20 min with polite delays)

# 3. Render this report
Rscript -e "rmarkdown::render('report.Rmd')"
```

**Pipeline outputs (all reproducible from `main.R`):**

```{r outputs-table, echo=FALSE}
tribble(
  ~File,                                   ~Records, ~Description,
  "`data/raw/lamoncloa_raw.csv`",          "1,078",  "All CDX-discovered La Moncloa press release URLs",
  "`data/raw/polls_raw.csv`",              "421",    "All Wikipedia opinion poll rows (raw)",
  "`data/raw/ine_demographics_raw.csv`",   "212+",   "INE provincial unemployment + foreign population (combined)",
  "`data/raw/elpais_headlines_raw.csv`",   "3,597+", "El País immigration + España article records",
  "`data/processed/immigration_statements.csv`", "63", "Confirmed La Moncloa immigration releases (with full text)",
  "`data/processed/monthly_statements.csv`","28",   "Monthly government immigration statement counts",
  "`data/processed/monthly_polls.csv`",   "28",    "Monthly PSOE/PP/Vox/Sumar poll averages (from 421 polls)",
  "`data/processed/salience_scores.csv`", "28",    "Monthly El País immigration media salience metrics",
  "`data/processed/master_panel.csv`",    "28",    "Full merged analysis panel (18 variables)",
  "`data/processed/model_results.csv`",   "4",     "OLS M3 coefficient table (Newey-West SEs)",
  "`data/processed/model_comparison.csv`","4",     "All model R², AIC, BIC comparison",
  "`data/processed/forecast_2027.csv`",   "48",    "PSOE forecast to June 2027 ,3 scenarios × 16 months",
  "`data/processed/seat_projections.csv`","3",    "D'Hondt seat projections ,3 scenarios (script 07)",
  "`data/processed/seat_monte_carlo.csv`","15000","Monte Carlo seat draws ,5,000 per scenario (script 07)",
  "`data/processed/var_forecast_2027.csv`","16",  "VAR bivariate (PSOE+PP) forecast to June 2027",
  "`data/processed/arima_prediction_se.csv`","16","ARIMA prediction SEs (derived from 95% CI)",
  "`data/processed/pp_psoe_correlation.csv`","1", "Empirical PP-PSOE polling correlation",
  "`data/processed/forecast_accuracy.csv`","4",   "Rolling one-step-ahead accuracy metrics (ARIMA vs ETS)",
  "`data/processed/diagnostics_summary.csv`","9", "Statistical diagnostics summary (ADF, PP, Ljung-Box, etc.)",
  "`data/processed/historical_polls_2004_2008.csv`","48","Monthly PSOE/PP polls 2004–2008 (script 08)",
  "`data/processed/its_results.csv`",    "4",     "ITS regression coefficients ,2005 regularisation effect (script 08)"
) |>
  kbl_proj(
    caption = "Table 22. Complete pipeline output reference",
    align   = c("l","r","l")
  )
```


---

# References

**Data Sources**

- INE (Instituto Nacional de Estadística). *Encuesta de Población Activa ,Tasas de actividad, paro y empleo por provincia y sexo (Table 65349)*. `servicios.ine.es/wstempus/js/ES/DATOS_TABLA/65349`
- INE. *Estadística Continua de Población ,Series CP305495 (total population) & CP305423 (Spanish population)*. `servicios.ine.es/wstempus/js/ES/DATOS_SERIE/`
- INE. *API Documentation (Data Access via Web Services)*. `www.ine.es/dyngs/DAB/en/index.htm`
- Internet Archive. *CDX Server API Documentation*. `github.com/internetarchive/wayback/tree/master/wayback-cdx-server`
- La Moncloa. *Notas de Prensa ,Ministerio del Interior, Ministerio de Inclusión, Seguridad Social y Migraciones; Ministerio de Asuntos Exteriores, Unión Europea y Cooperación*. `lamoncloa.gob.es/serviciosdeprensa/notasprensa/`
- El País. *Noticias de Inmigración*. `elpais.com/noticias/inmigracion/`
- Ministerio de Inclusión, Seguridad Social y Migraciones (2026). *Proceso de regularización extraordinaria 2026 ,Iniciativa Legislativa Popular*. La Moncloa press releases, January 2026.
- Wikipedia. *"Opinion polling for the next Spanish general election"*. Retrieved February 2026. `en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Opinion_polling_for_the_next_Spanish_general_election`
- Wikipedia. *"Opinion polling for the 2008 Spanish general election"*. Retrieved February 2026. `en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Opinion_polling_for_the_2008_Spanish_general_election`
- Ministerio del Interior. *Resultados electorales ,Elecciones Generales 23 de julio de 2023*. `infoelectoral.mir.es`

**Statistical and Methodological References**

- Andrews, D.W.K. (1991). Heteroskedasticity and autocorrelation consistent covariance matrix estimation. *Econometrica*, 59(3), 817–858.
- Egan, P.J. (2013). *Partisan Priorities: How Issue Ownership Drives and Distorts American Politics*. Cambridge University Press.
- Erikson, R.S. (1988). The puzzle of midterm loss. *Journal of Politics*, 50(4), 1011–1029.
- Granger, C.W.J. (1969). Investigating causal relations by econometric models and cross-spectral methods. *Econometrica*, 37(3), 424–438.
- Hyndman, R.J. & Athanasopoulos, G. (2021). *Forecasting: Principles and Practice* (3rd ed.). OTexts. `otexts.com/fpp3`
- Nannestad, P. & Paldam, M. (1994). The VP-function: A survey of the literature on vote and popularity functions after 25 years. *Public Choice*, 79(3–4), 213–245.
- Newey, W.K. & West, K.D. (1987). A simple, positive semi-definite, heteroskedasticity and autocorrelation consistent covariance matrix. *Econometrica*, 55(3), 703–708.
- Petrocik, J.R. (1996). Issue ownership in presidential elections, with a 1980 case study. *American Journal of Political Science*, 40(3), 825–850.
- Shadish, W.R., Cook, T.D. & Campbell, D.T. (2002). *Experimental and Quasi-Experimental Designs for Generalized Causal Inference*. Houghton Mifflin. [ITS methodology]
- Wagner, A.K., Soumerai, S.B., Zhang, F. & Ross-Degnan, D. (2002). Segmented regression analysis of interrupted time series studies in medication use research. *Journal of Clinical Pharmacy and Therapeutics*, 27(4), 299–309.

**Spain-Specific Sources**

- BBC News (2026). *Spain launches biggest migrant amnesty since 2005*. BBC Online, January 2026.
- CIS ,Centro de Investigaciones Sociológicas (2024–2025). *Barómetro mensual ,preocupaciones principales*. `cis.es/baro`
- Fraile, M. & Lewis-Beck, M.S. (2014). Economic voting in Spain: a 2008 panel test. *European Journal of Political Research*, 53(1), 1–16. [used to corroborate 2004–2008 poll digitisation]
- Junta Electoral Central. *Ley Orgánica del Régimen Electoral General (LOREG), Artículo 163 ,Atribución de escaños*. `boe.es`
- Maldita.es (2026). *¿Los migrantes regularizados en 2026 podrán votar en las elecciones de 2027?* February 2026.
- Ministerio del Interior (2024–2025). *Balances de llegadas de inmigrantes irregulares*. `interior.gob.es`

---

*Report generated with R `r R.version$major`.`r R.version$minor` · `r format(Sys.Date(), "%d %B %Y")`*

*UC3M Data Harvesting Course ,Team: Maimaitiming, Varberg Sabri, Tadmuri · Instructor: Patricia Callejo*
